text.content: "You read and created bar graphs with scale of 10. You figured out values at the lines and between the lines. Let's look at some patterns you discovered."

rich_text.plain_text: "You read and created bar graphs with scale of 10. You figured out values at the lines and between the lines. Let's look at some patterns you discovered."

rich_text.plain_text: 1.1  Scale Progression Pattern Type Pattern  [s1_1_scale_progression_pattern_type_pattern]

text.content: Vertical bar graph with scale of 10. Single bar showing value of 20. Axis shows 0, 10, 20. 2 gridlines visible.

rich_text.plain_text: Vertical bar graph with scale of 10. Single bar showing value of 20. Axis shows 0, 10, 20. 2 gridlines visible.

text.content: "Look at all four graphs. They each only show one bar, but that bar on every graph shows the same amount: 20. Which scale uses the fewest lines to show the same amount?"

rich_text.plain_text: "Look at all four graphs. They each only show one bar, but that bar on every graph shows the same amount: 20. Which scale uses the fewest lines to show the same amount?"

text.content:
tool: click_tangible  target: bar_graph_scale_1, bar_graph_scale_2, bar_graph_scale_5, bar_graph_scale_10
"Which scale uses the fewest lines to show the same amount?"

tool: click_tangible  target: bar_graph_scale_1, bar_graph_scale_2, bar_graph_scale_5, bar_graph_scale_10

text.content: ✅ Student clicked scale of 10 graph (rightmost), correct

text.content: "Yes. Scale of 10 uses the fewest lines. Each line shows more, so you need fewer of them."

rich_text.plain_text: "Yes. Scale of 10 uses the fewest lines. Each line shows more, so you need fewer of them."

text.content: ❌ Student clicked a wrong graph on second attempt

text.content: Scale of 10 graph highlights.

rich_text.plain_text: Scale of 10 graph highlights.

text.content: "Let's count the axis lines together. Start from the bottom and go up to where the bar stops at 20. Scale of 1 has 20 lines, one at every number. Scale of 2 has 10 lines: 2, 4, 6, 8, up to 20. Check Scale of 5 and Scale of 10. Which graph has the fewest lines to reach 20?"

rich_text.plain_text: "Let's count the axis lines together. Start from the bottom and go up to where the bar stops at 20. Scale of 1 has 20 lines, one at every number. Scale of 2 has 10 lines: 2, 4, 6, 8, up to 20. Check Scale of 5 and Scale of 10. Which graph has the fewest lines to reach 20?"

text.content: ❌ Student clicked a wrong graph three or more times. System models the answer.

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student clicked a wrong graph three or more times. System models the answer.

text.content: Scale of 10 graph highlights to show correct answer.

rich_text.plain_text: Scale of 10 graph highlights to show correct answer.

text.content: "Let’s look at the rest of the graphs together. We saw Scale of 1 has 20 lines and Scale of 2 has 10 lines. Let’s check Scale of 5 and Scale of 10. Scale of 5 has 4 lines: 5, 10, 15, 20. Scale of 10 has 2 lines: 10, 20. The graph with scale of 10 has only 2 lines to reach 20, that's fewer than any other scale. When each line counts for more, you need fewer lines."

rich_text.plain_text: "Let’s look at the rest of the graphs together. We saw Scale of 1 has 20 lines and Scale of 2 has 10 lines. Let’s check Scale of 5 and Scale of 10. Scale of 5 has 4 lines: 5, 10, 15, 20. Scale of 10 has 2 lines: 10, 20. The graph with scale of 10 has only 2 lines to reach 20, that's fewer than any other scale. When each line counts for more, you need fewer lines."

text.content: ⏭️ Student presses Next

rich_text.plain_text: ⏭️ Student presses Next

text.content: "You've learned four scales now: 1, 2, 5, and 10. Each one is useful for different sized numbers. Bigger numbers work better with bigger scales."

rich_text.plain_text: "You've learned four scales now: 1, 2, 5, and 10. Each one is useful for different sized numbers. Bigger numbers work better with bigger scales."

text.content: "A scale of 10 is useful when numbers are bigger. Where might you see counting by 10s outside of math class? Select ALL that would work well."

rich_text.plain_text: "A scale of 10 is useful when numbers are bigger. Where might you see counting by 10s outside of math class? Select ALL that would work well."

text.content:
tool: multi_select  options: ["A", "B", "C"]
"Which situations would work well with a scale of 10? Select ALL that apply."

rich_text.plain_text:
tool: multi_select  options: ["A", "B", "C"]
"Which situations would work well with a scale of 10? Select ALL that apply."

text.content: "Let’s think through this together.

text.content: Image A

text.content: shows money. Bills come in tens, so we count by 10s. $10, $20, $30. Image B shows a ruler. This centimeter ruler has marks at 10, 20, 30. Both use a scale of 10. Image C counts one by one, not by 10s, so that is not a scale of 10. Money and measuring often use 10s. That's why a scale of 10 feels natural for bigger amounts."

Image C counts one by one, not by 10s, so that is not a scale of 10. Money and measuring often use 10s. That's why a scale of 10 feels natural for bigger amounts."

text.content: "You already know how to count by 10s. Bar graphs use the same skill you use with money and measuring."

text.content: 4.1  Metacognitive Reflection Strategy Identification  [s4_1_metacognitive_reflection_strategy_identification]

rich_text.plain_text: 4.1  Metacognitive Reflection Strategy Identification  [s4_1_metacognitive_reflection_strategy_identification]

text.content: Vertical bar graph appears. Single bar labeled Data ends at 45, between 40 and 50 gridlines. Bar highlighted. Ghost gridlines at 5s visible, showing halfway marks between each pair of labeled gridlines.

rich_text.plain_text: Vertical bar graph appears. Single bar labeled Data ends at 45, between 40 and 50 gridlines. Bar highlighted. Ghost gridlines at 5s visible, showing halfway marks between each pair of labeled gridlines.

text.content: "This bar shows 45, between the lines. How did you figure that out the value during the lesson?"

rich_text.plain_text: "This bar shows 45, between the lines. How did you figure that out the value during the lesson?"

text.content:
tool: multiple_choice  options: ["I picked the line closest to the top of the bar", "I added the two numbers on the lines", "I found halfway, which is always 5 more", "I guessed"]
"What strategy helped you find values between the lines?"

rich_text.plain_text:
tool: multiple_choice  options: ["I picked the line closest to the top of the bar", "I added the two numbers on the lines", "I found halfway, which is always 5 more", "I guessed"]
"What strategy helped you find values between the lines?"

text.content: ✅ Student selected the halfway strategy, recognizing the pattern that halfway between lines on scale 10 is always 5 more

rich_text.plain_text: ✅ Student selected the halfway strategy, recognizing the pattern that halfway between lines on scale 10 is always 5 more

text.content: "Right. Half of 10 is 5, so halfway between any two lines is 5 more than the lower line."

rich_text.plain_text: "Right. Half of 10 is 5, so halfway between any two lines is 5 more than the lower line."

text.content: ❌ Student selected the closest-line strategy, which gives approximate rather than precise values

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student selected the closest-line strategy, which gives approximate rather than precise values

text.content: Ghost gridlines at 5s pulse to show halfway marks between labeled lines

rich_text.plain_text: Ghost gridlines at 5s pulse to show halfway marks between labeled lines

text.content: "Picking the closest line helps when it is closer to one line and you estimate, but this bar ends exactly halfway between 40 and 50. What strategy can help you find the exact value?"

rich_text.plain_text: "Picking the closest line helps when it is closer to one line and you estimate, but this bar ends exactly halfway between 40 and 50. What strategy can help you find the exact value?"

text.content: ❌ Student selected adding the gridline numbers, which doesn't work for finding values between lines

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student selected adding the gridline numbers, which doesn't work for finding values between lines

text.content: Ghost gridlines at 5s pulse. The bar top aligns with the halfway gridline between 40 and 50

rich_text.plain_text: Ghost gridlines at 5s pulse. The bar top aligns with the halfway gridline between 40 and 50

text.content: "Adding 40 and 50 gives 90, not 45. This bar does not go up to 90. The bar sits halfway between 40 and 50. What other strategy can you use to find its value?"

rich_text.plain_text: "Adding 40 and 50 gives 90, not 45. This bar does not go up to 90. The bar sits halfway between 40 and 50. What other strategy can you use to find its value?"

text.content: ❌ Student selected guessing, missing the systematic halfway strategy

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student selected guessing, missing the systematic halfway strategy

text.content: Ghost gridlines at 5s pulse to highlight the halfway pattern

rich_text.plain_text: Ghost gridlines at 5s pulse to highlight the halfway pattern

text.content: "You don't need to guess. The bar sits exactly in the middle between 40 and 50. The scale is 10, so what value would be in the middle? What strategy can you use to find the exact value without guessing?"

rich_text.plain_text: "You don't need to guess. The bar sits exactly in the middle between 40 and 50. The scale is 10, so what value would be in the middle? What strategy can you use to find the exact value without guessing?"

text.content: ❌ Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

text.content: The halfway gridline at 45 pulses, then a visual shows 40 + 5 = 45

rich_text.plain_text: The halfway gridline at 45 pulses, then a visual shows 40 + 5 = 45

text.content: "Let’s review the strategy to find values between the lines. The scale is 10, so lines on the axis go 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50. Halfway between any two lines is 5 more than the lower one, half of 10. Between 40 and 50, halfway is 45. That's 40 plus 5. The bar ends exactly at that halfway point, so the value is 45. Finding halfway between lines is the strategy that works every time with scale of 10."

rich_text.plain_text: "Let’s review the strategy to find values between the lines. The scale is 10, so lines on the axis go 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50. Halfway between any two lines is 5 more than the lower one, half of 10. Between 40 and 50, halfway is 45. That's 40 plus 5. The bar ends exactly at that halfway point, so the value is 45. Finding halfway between lines is the strategy that works every time with scale of 10."

text.content: ⏭️ Student presses Next

rich_text.plain_text: ⏭️ Student presses Next

text.content: "Knowing that pattern means you can read any bar graph with scale of 10, even values between the lines. That's a powerful strategy."

rich_text.plain_text: "Knowing that pattern means you can read any bar graph with scale of 10, even values between the lines. That's a powerful strategy."

text.content: ⏭️ Student presses Next — toggle open to see current scene state

rich_text.plain_text: ⏭️ Student presses Next — toggle open to see current scene state

text.content: Vertical bar graph in reading mode. Single Data bar at value 45, highlighted. Ghost gridlines visible at 5s.

rich_text.plain_text: Vertical bar graph in reading mode. Single Data bar at value 45, highlighted. Ghost gridlines visible at 5s.

text.content: "You learned to read values at the lines and between the lines. You used the fact that half of 10 is 5 to figure out tricky values."

rich_text.plain_text: "You learned to read values at the lines and between the lines. You used the fact that half of 10 is 5 to figure out tricky values."

text.content: "That pattern-finding will help you when we look at all the scales together next time. You'll learn to pick the best one for any data."

rich_text.plain_text: "That pattern-finding will help you when we look at all the scales together next time. You'll learn to pick the best one for any data."
